questions.question: Explain the difference between scalar and vector quantities with examples from motion.
questions.answer: Scalar quantities have only magnitude while vector quantities have both magnitude and direction. In motion, distance and speed are scalars whereas displacement and velocity are vectors.

questions.question: Explain the importance of reference point in describing motion.
questions.answer: Motion is described relative to a reference point. An object may appear moving with respect to one point but stationary with respect to another.

questions.question: Explain how distance and displacement are measured in real life situations.
questions.answer: Distance is measured along the path travelled while displacement is measured as the straight-line distance between initial and final points.

questions.question: Explain the characteristics of uniform motion.
questions.answer: Uniform motion occurs when equal distances are travelled in equal intervals of time and the speed remains constant.

questions.question: Explain the characteristics of non-uniform motion.
questions.answer: Non-uniform motion occurs when unequal distances are covered in equal intervals of time and speed changes continuously.

questions.question: Explain how average speed differs from instantaneous speed.
questions.answer: Average speed is the total distance divided by total time while instantaneous speed is the speed at a particular instant.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between speed, velocity and acceleration.
questions.answer: Speed measures how fast an object moves, velocity adds direction to speed, and acceleration measures how velocity changes with time.

questions.question: Explain the SI units used for distance, speed and acceleration.
questions.answer: Distance is measured in metre (m), speed in metre per second (m/s) and acceleration in metre per second squared (m/s²).

questions.question: Explain how graphs are useful in understanding motion.
questions.answer: Graphs help visualize motion and allow calculation of speed, acceleration and displacement.

questions.question: Explain the slope of a distance-time graph.
questions.answer: The slope of a distance-time graph represents the speed of the object.

questions.question: Explain the significance of slope in velocity-time graph.
questions.answer: The slope of the velocity-time graph represents acceleration.

questions.question: Explain the area under velocity-time graph.
questions.answer: The area under a velocity-time graph gives the displacement of the object.

questions.question: Explain the first equation of motion.
questions.answer: The first equation of motion is v = u + at which relates velocity, acceleration and time.

questions.question: Explain the second equation of motion.
questions.answer: The second equation of motion is s = ut + ½at² which relates displacement with time and acceleration.

questions.question: Explain the third equation of motion.
questions.answer: The third equation of motion is v² − u² = 2as which relates velocity, acceleration and displacement.

questions.question: Explain the conditions under which equations of motion are valid.
questions.answer: They are valid when acceleration is uniform and motion occurs in a straight line.

questions.question: Explain uniform circular motion with examples.
questions.answer: Uniform circular motion is motion along a circular path with constant speed such as motion of Earth around the Sun.

questions.question: Explain why circular motion is accelerated motion.
questions.answer: Even though speed remains constant, velocity changes due to change in direction.

questions.question: Explain how speed of vehicles is measured.
questions.answer: Speed of vehicles is measured using a speedometer.

questions.question: Explain how distance travelled by vehicles is recorded.
questions.answer: Distance travelled by vehicles is recorded using an odometer.

questions.question: Explain how motion can be classified based on path.
questions.answer: Motion can be classified into linear motion, circular motion and oscillatory motion.

questions.question: Explain why displacement may be zero even when distance is not zero.
questions.answer: When an object returns to its starting point, displacement becomes zero while distance travelled remains positive.

questions.question: Explain how acceleration affects motion.
questions.answer: Acceleration changes the velocity of an object either by increasing, decreasing speed or changing direction.

questions.question: Explain why studying motion is important in physics.
questions.answer: Studying motion helps understand movement of objects and is useful in transportation and engineering.

questions.question: Explain the motion of satellites around Earth.
questions.answer: Satellites revolve around Earth in circular paths due to gravitational force showing uniform circular motion.
